preFix.title: Making a Simple Press Mould from Air-Dry Clay

content.text: A
press mould

lets you reproduce the same shallow dish, tray, or tile form over and over without measuring each time. Made from air-dry clay and left to harden fully before use, it behaves much like a bisque-fired plaster mould: porous enough to draw a little moisture away from the pressed piece, helping it release cleanly. The key is building a form with a gentle
draft angle
on all walls so pressed pieces pull free without tearing.

tools.name: Soft brush or pastry brush for cornflour

content.text: Preparation

Decide on the shape you want to press. A shallow oval dish 12-15 cm across with sides no higher than 25 mm is a good starting size. The mould needs to be at least 20 mm thick at its base and 15 mm thick at its walls to survive repeated pressing without cracking. Allow 3-5 days of drying time before you use the finished mould.

Build the mould form as a solid lump of clay in the positive shape you want to reproduce. For a shallow dish, model a low dome with gently sloping sides. Make sure every wall angles outward slightly from top to bottom, giving the draft angle that allows pressed pieces to release. Pinch, scrape, and smooth until the form is exactly the shape you want the inside of the finished dish to be.

Smooth the outside of the mould very well using a wooden rib. Any texture on the mould surface will transfer to every piece you press from it. Work methodically, pulling the rib across in overlapping strokes until the surface is even and slightly shiny from compression.

Set the mould on a flat board to dry slowly. Cover loosely with a cloth for the first day to slow the drying and reduce the risk of cracking. After the first day, uncover and leave to dry completely, at least 48-3 days. The mould is ready when it is hard all the way through, pale in colour, and noticeably lighter than when it was fresh.
Before each pressing session, dust the mould surface generously with cornflour using a soft brush. Cornflour is the
release agent
that prevents the fresh clay from bonding to the mould surface. Tap off any excess so you have a thin, even coating rather than a pile in the centre.

Roll out a fresh slab of clay about 6-8 mm thick. Drape it gently over the mould, starting from the centre and working outward to avoid trapping air. Use your fingers and a wooden rib to press the clay firmly against the mould surface, keeping even pressure throughout.
Trim the excess clay from the rim using the fettling knife, cutting along the base of the mould to give a clean edge. Smooth the cut edge with a damp finger.

Leave the pressed piece on the mould for 20-40 minutes until it stiffens enough to hold its shape. Lift one edge gently to check: the clay should release from the mould without stretching. If it still clings, wait another 10 minutes. Once released, place the dish right-side up on a flat board to finish drying.
What success looks like

The mould releases cleanly each time with no tearing. Pressed dishes are consistent in depth and curve. The inner surface of each dish reflects the smooth finish of the mould. Re-dusting with cornflour between each pressing keeps the process running smoothly.
Common mistakes
Using the mould before it is fully dry: a damp mould will bond to the pressed clay and cause tearing on release.
Skipping the draft angle: vertical or inward-sloping walls trap the pressed piece and make it impossible to remove cleanly.
Not re-dusting between pressings: the cornflour absorbs into the mould surface over time and must be reapplied every two or three presses.

items.fix: Apply more cornflour to the mould and wait until the clay has firmed to leather-hard before attempting to lift it free.
items.cause: Clay released too early or mould surface not dusted adequately
items.problem: Pressed piece tears when lifted from mould

items.fix: Patch cracks with fresh clay and re-dry slowly under a cloth. For the next mould, keep walls at least 15 mm thick and dry in a cool room away from drafts.
items.cause: Walls too thin or drying happened too quickly

items.fix: Start from the centre of the mould and work outward, smoothing firmly with a rib as you go to push air toward the edges before it gets trapped.
items.cause: Air trapped between the slab and the mould when draping
items.problem: Pressed dish has air bubbles or uneven surface
